title: Improving Breakfast Flow Through Occupancy Visibility & Flexible Booking
shortDescription: A QR-based booking system that helps hotel guests avoid crowded breakfast hours using real-time availability and flexible booking.
tagline: A hospitality UX concept designed to reduce breakfast congestion through real-time occupancy visibility and flexible reservation interactions.
overview: During peak breakfast hours, guests arriving at the same time often cause overcrowding, long wait times, and **service breakdown**.
introduction: Based on my experience working in gastronomy and hotel environments, I observed how peak hours often become overcrowded, leading to long wait times, overwhelmed staff, and a poor guest experience.
problemStatement:
Guests tend to arrive at similar times due to a lack of visibility into occupancy, leading to overcrowding and frustration.

At the same time, staff lack tools to manage demand and distribute guest flow effectively.
keyInsight: Guests tend to choose the most convenient visible option rather than planning ahead, which leads to clustering during peak times.
processIntro: I prioritised influencing user behaviour through real-time availability and visual cues, instead of enforcing strict booking rules.

uxui.context:
During busy breakfast hours, guests often experience overcrowding, long waiting times, and inconsistent buffet availability.

The goal was to create a smoother and less stressful breakfast experience through better occupancy visibility and lightweight reservation interactions, while also reducing operational pressure on staff during peak periods.
uxui.userPainPoints: Guests tend to choose familiar breakfast times without clear visibility into real-time occupancy patterns|Peak-time congestion creates frustration for both guests and staff|Guests trying to avoid crowded periods often lack flexible alternatives|Traditional reservation systems often feel too rigid for spontaneous guest behaviour|Staff experience increased operational pressure during high-demand periods
uxui.uxGoal: Design an optional and intuitive booking experience that helps guests make better timing decisions without introducing unnecessary friction or restrictive reservation rules.
uxui.uxDecisions: ##Occupancy Visibility##Used simple occupancy states (quiet, steady, full) with colour indicators to make crowd levels immediately understandable at a glance.##img##/resources/Bft-App/OccupancyColours.png|##Low-Friction Booking Flow##Reduced unnecessary steps to keep the reservation process quick and approachable during fast decision-making moments.##imgleft##/resources/Bft-App/Flow7.png|##Alternative Suggestions##When certain time slots become unavailable, nearby alternatives remain clearly visible and easy to select, helping users continue the booking flow without unnecessary frustration.##imgsmall##/resources/Bft-App/Booking.png|##Mobile-First Interaction##Designed primarily for mobile use, since guests are likely to access the system quickly through QR codes within the hotel environment.##imgleft2##/resources/Bft-App/LandingPage0.png;/resources/Bft-App/LandingPage1.png|##Cross-Platform Exploration##A desktop overview was also explored to support guests accessing the system through laptops or in-room devices.##imgbelow2##/resources/Bft-App/Overview-iPhoneMockup.png;/resources/Bft-App/Desktop-Overview1.png
uxui.userFlow:
The experience guides users from QR scan to reservation confirmation through a minimal number of decisions.

Each step was designed to reduce cognitive load while maintaining visibility into availability and flexibility.
uxui.accessibilityClarity: The interface prioritises large touch targets, clear visual hierarchy, and glanceable occupancy indicators to support fast interaction in real-world hospitality environments.
uxui.learnings: Users respond more naturally to visible guidance than enforced restrictions|Reducing friction can improve adoption in operational environments|Balancing flexibility with operational reliability is essential in service-based systems|UX decisions strongly influence perceived service quality during high-stress moments
uxui.finalImpact: The concept redistributes breakfast demand across time slots, helping reduce congestion while improving both guest experience and operational flow.

processNarrative.description:
Designed a QR-based booking system that redistributes demand by guiding guests toward less crowded time slots using real-time availability and visual cues. Guests can explore occupancy levels, identify peak times, and optionally reserve a slot while retaining flexibility.

Instead of controlling users, the system guides their decisions to improve overall flow.

Guests access the system via QR code, view breakfast availability, and select a preferred time slot. The system validates availability in real time and suggests alternatives if a slot is unavailable. After booking, guests can manage their reservation by modifying or canceling it as needed.
The system validates availability in real time and suggests alternative time slots when capacity is reached.

learnings: Learned that user behaviour in real environments is driven more by visible options than planning.|Designing for flow requires influencing decisions, not just providing functionality.|Balanced user flexibility with operational constraints to ensure reliable outcomes.
impact: Redistributes demand across time slots, reduces peak-time congestion, and improves both guest experience and operational efficiency.
metrics: Visible availability states (quiet, steady, full) make occupancy easy to understand
Alternative suggestions keep users moving forward instead of blocking interaction
A lightweight booking flow reduces friction and encourages adoption
Flexibility is maintained to balance user choice with operational constraints

title: Reducing Scheduling Conflicts in Hospitality Through Real-Time Shift Management
shortDescription: Coordinating staff schedules while allowing flexible shift swaps with manager approval.
overview: While working in hospitality, I observed that shift coordination relied on manual communication, making it difficult to manage changes, preferences, and last-minute updates.
introduction: While working in gastronomy and hotel environments, I experienced firsthand how shift coordination often relies on manual communication, making it difficult to manage swaps, preferences, and last-minute changes.
problemStatement:
Shift management is often handled through static schedules or informal channels (e.g. WhatsApp, printed rosters), leading to:

• Lack of real-time visibility

• High coordination effort for managers

• Frequent miscommunication

This leads to understaffed shifts, increased workload for managers, and frustration for staff.
keyInsight: Most issues don’t come from the schedule itself, but from last-minute changes and the lack of real-time updates.
designGoal: I prioritised real-time updates and automated validation over static scheduling to reduce coordination overhead and prevent operational failures.
hypothesis:
Designed a mobile-first system enabling staff to manage shifts and request swaps in real time, while managers maintain reliable staffing through automated validation and approval workflows.

This approach reduces manual coordination and improves reliability in high-pressure hospitality environments.
tradeoffs: A fully flexible system would increase user freedom but risk understaffing and service disruption.
processIntro: Structured shift swap and validation logic through user flows, then tested interaction patterns using low- and mid-fidelity wireframes.
learnings: Designed for multiple user roles, balancing staff flexibility with managerial control.|Simplified complex operational rules (e.g. shift validation) into clear, intuitive interactions.|Learned that most scheduling issues stem from real-time changes, not initial planning.
metrics: Prioritised simplicity over flexibility to reduce coordination effort in high-pressure environments
Introduced rule-based validation to prevent understaffing and ensure reliable shift coverage
Designed role-based workflows to balance staff flexibility with managerial control
Used clear system feedback to help users understand outcomes and reduce confusion
